clarification: Multiple related APIs and Matter behaviors allowing a guest to obtain fabric credentials, enumerate node IDs, add malicious node IDs, and escalate to fabric admin via issued certificates.

entries.Expected Behavior: Only fabric administrators (or other authorized roles) may call this API. The API should validate caller permissions and only return RCAC/ICAC certificates, fabric ID, and node ID when the caller is authorized for that fabric.
entries.Buggy Behavior: A normal/guest-level user can call the API and receive valid RCAC/ICAC certificates, the fabric ID, and an attacker-assigned node ID, enabling the attacker to obtain fabric credentials and act as a controller/admin for that fabric.
entries.clarification: Attacker can obtain fabric certificates tied to the home's fabric, enabling use of CHIP Tool as a controller.

entries.Argument Info: Request with { "gid": <group-id>, ... } or other identifiers to enumerate node IDs in a fabric
entries.Expected Behavior: Only authorized fabric controllers or users with appropriate view permissions may enumerate device node IDs; the API should restrict or obfuscate node identifiers to prevent enumeration by low-privilege users.
entries.Buggy Behavior: A guest/low-privilege user can call the API and obtain the list of node IDs for devices in the fabric, allowing enumeration and mapping of owner/device node IDs.
entries.clarification: Allows mapping of node IDs which attackers can use to hijack or impersonate devices.

entries.Argument Info: ACL write payload (List or Map) e.g. [{ "fabricindex": <int>, "privilege": <int>, "authMode": <int>, "subjects": [<ids>], "targets": <null|list> }]
entries.Expected Behavior: Only the fabric administrator (or properly authorized subjects) may write or modify a device's ACL. ACL entries must be validated and limited to prevent granting admin-level privileges to unauthorized subjects.
entries.Buggy Behavior: An attacker with temporary/guest access can write a new ACL that grants high privilege (e.g., privilege 5) to attacker-controlled subjects (including malicious node IDs), allowing remote control and privilege escalation; the owner may be demoted or lose control.
entries.clarification: ACL writing abused to escalate privileges and persist control.

entries.Command: Create/Add Matter device to fabric with Node ID (commissioning)
entries.Argument Info: Commissioning request parameters (fabric ID, Node ID, commissioning payload); Node ID supplied/controlled by controller during commissioning
entries.Expected Behavior: The fabric controller or provisioning flow should prevent a non-admin from adding devices with arbitrary Node IDs that collide with existing or predicted Node IDs; Node ID assignment should be controlled and checked server- and device-side to prevent collisions and spoofing.
entries.Buggy Behavior: An attacker can add a malicious device specifying an arbitrary (or predicted) Node ID X into the fabric; this can cause command routing conflicts and allow the attacker to intercept or hijack control of benign devices that later receive the same Node ID.
entries.clarification: Node ID collisions allow hijacking of control when owner later gets same Node ID.

entries.Argument Info: Behavioral property: node IDs assigned to users/devices are incremental (predictable) within a home/fabric
entries.Expected Behavior: Node IDs for users and devices should not be trivially enumerable or predictably incremental; APIs and assignments must avoid leaking a simple sequence that allows brute-force prediction.
entries.Buggy Behavior: Node IDs are assigned incrementally and/or disclosed by APIs. A temporary guest can enumerate or predict the owner/device Node ID by brute forcing the predictable sequence and then impersonate them or pre-claim the next device Node ID.
entries.clarification: Incremental node IDs leak strong predictive information.

entries.Command: Use CHIP Tool with stolen fabric credentials
entries.Argument Info: Attacker uses obtained fabric certificates, fabric ID and node IDs (from thing.m.matter.icac.issue and device.node.batch.get) with CHIP Tool commands to act as a controller
entries.Expected Behavior: Obtaining certificates/credentials should be restricted so that only authorized administrators keep controller privileges. Possession of issued certificates by a low-privilege actor should not permit unilateral takeover of a fabric.
entries.Buggy Behavior: An attacker uses certificates and node IDs obtained as a guest to operate CHIP Tool and assume fabric admin/controller roles, enabling persistent control of devices (even after their app-level access is revoked).
entries.clarification: Fabric credentials enable continued control even after user revocation.

entries.Command: ACL modification resulting in DoS (combined attack)
entries.Argument Info: Sequence: (1) issue certificates (thing.m.matter.icac.issue), (2) add malicious device or change ACL via Matter AccessControl cluster write
entries.Expected Behavior: ACL modification flows must be restricted to authorized admins; owner ACL entries cannot be silently downgraded by a low-privileged actor. The system must log and notify owners when privileged ACL changes occur.
entries.Buggy Behavior: An attacker escalates privileges or modifies ACL such that the legitimate owner is demoted or removed; owner can no longer control or commission the device and receives no explanatory UI feedback (resulting in effective DoS).
entries.clarification: Combined sequence yields persistent DoS / admin-takeover.

title: Unlimited AppendItem in User Label Cluster causing DoS
reproduction_steps: Write a user label list with a large number of entries using chip-tool:
Observe CPU usage and responsiveness: CPU reaches 100%, processing times increase exponentially, resulting in timeouts. Reading back the list also times out.
summary: The WriteLabelList implementation allows unlimited AppendItem operations bypassing kMaxUserLabelListLength (10), causing resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, storage), timeouts, and system-wide slowdown / DoS.
root_cause_analysis: AppendItem path validates individual entry but does not enforce total list length. AppendUserLabel increments and stores entries without checking max length.
proposed_code_fix_snippet: Add check in AppendUserLabel to return CHIP_ERROR_NO_MEMORY if length >= kMaxUserLabelListLength before appending.

steps.send: Repeatedly send AppendItem entries via WriteLabelList with many items
steps.clarification: Flood the device with AppendItem requests to grow stored labels beyond intended limits.
Device appends entries without enforcing total maximum length.

facts.name: UnlimitedLabelAppend
facts.clarification: Labels appended past the expected maximum list size.

facts.name: ResourceExhausted
facts.clarification: Device CPU/memory/disk exhausted leading to timeouts and DoS.

rules.name: AppendWithoutLimit
rules.clarification: AppendItem path appends without checking global limit.

rules.name: ResourceExhaustion
rules.clarification: Unbounded growth leads to CPU/memory/disk exhaustion and timeouts.

lemmas.name: AppendCanCauseDoS
lemmas.clarification: Shows that an attacker can produce resource exhaustion via AppendItem operations.

initial_facts.clarification: Device starts with normal limited user label list (kMaxUserLabelListLength = 10).

title: Null pointer deref via malformed TCP Matter packet length (DoS)
reproduction_steps: Send a crafted TCP packet where the 4-byte length field is present but leads Consume() to free the only buffer (e.g., length exactly equals current buffer length and chain not present).
Observe crash logs showing timeout and null pointer dereference in ProcessSingleMessage.
summary: TCP-based Matter packet parsing reads a 4-byte length, consumes those bytes, and then calls ProcessSingleMessage without verifying that the receive buffer pointer is non-null; in some cases Consume frees the only buffer and returns nullptr, causing a null pointer deref and crash.
root_cause_analysis: After Read() returns the length and Consume(kPacketSizeBytes) is called, PacketBuffer::FreeHead can return nullptr, which is assigned to state->mReceived.mBuffer. ProcessSingleMessage dereferences state->mReceived without IsNull check.
proposed_mitigation_snippet: After Consume(kPacketSizeBytes); call VerifyOrReturnError(!state->mReceived.IsNull(), CHIP_ERROR_MESSAGE_INCOMPLETE); before calling ProcessSingleMessage.

steps.clarification: Attacker sends a single unauthenticated TCP Matter packet crafted so that Consume() frees the only buffer — causing state->mReceived to become null.
Device consumes packet length and then calls ProcessSingleMessage without checking state->mReceived for null, leading to crash.

facts.name: BufferFreed
facts.clarification: Internal TCP buffer chain freed resulting in null mBuffer pointer.

facts.name: NullDerefCrash
facts.clarification: Null pointer dereference in ProcessSingleMessage causing application crash and DoS.

rules.name: MalformedTCPPacketAccepted
rules.clarification: Malformed TCP packet length leads Consume() to free buffer chain and return nullptr.

rules.name: ProcessSingleMessageNullCrash
rules.clarification: ProcessSingleMessage dereferences state->mReceived without null check, causing crash.

lemmas.name: NullPointerCrashReachable
lemmas.clarification: Demonstrates that a crash (DoS) is reachable via a single unauthenticated malformed TCP packet.

initial_facts.clarification: Device with a TCP listener exists initially.

title: WriteAcl ReplaceAll then decode failure leaves ACL empty (DoS)
reproduction_steps: After pairing, read ACL to confirm existing entries:
Send a write request that includes invalid entry (e.g., privilege 99):
Observe response failure (CONSTRAINT_ERROR) but ACL entries deleted and left empty.
summary: WriteAcl implementation deletes all existing ACL entries (ReplaceAll) and then decodes & recreates entries. If TLV decoding fails mid-way, the function aborts and leaves the ACL empty, causing denial of access including administrator.
root_cause_analysis: TLV decoded sequentially. ReplaceAll semantics delete first; invalid entries cause decoding to fail after deletion; no rollback or staging is used.

steps.clarification: Attacker or mis-sent write that contains invalid field causing constraint error during decoding.
Device deletes existing ACLs then fails to decode new ACL entries, leaving the ACL empty.

facts.name: ACLDeleted
facts.clarification: Represents the state after ReplaceAll where all ACL entries are removed.

facts.name: DecodeFailed
facts.clarification: TLV decoding failed due to invalid input; no new ACL entries were added.

facts.name: ACLDoS
facts.clarification: Device is left with no ACL entries, causing complete access denial for all users including administrators.

rules.name: ProcessReplaceAll
rules.clarification: WriteAcl deletes all existing ACL entries before validating incoming data.

rules.name: DecodeFailureAfterReplace
rules.clarification: TLV decoding of the new entries fails because the payload contains invalid values (e.g., privilege=99).

rules.name: FinalACLDoS
rules.clarification: The decode failure aborts recreation, leaving the ACL list empty and the device inaccessible.

lemmas.name: ACLDoSIsReachable
lemmas.clarification: Shows that a user-provided invalid WriteAcl request triggers full ACL deletion and results in permanent denial of access.

initial_facts.clarification: Device begins in a normal state with valid ACL entries.

title: In-Fabric Attribute Disclosure (Footprinting)
summary: Implementation issue in connectedhomeip SDK (Matter 1.2) allows a low-privileged in-fabric actor to disclose attributes of other devices (footprinting) that should be restricted.

steps.clarification: Attacker part of same fabric sends attribute read or crafted queries to infer metadata.
Due to insufficient isolation, device processes and leaks attribute metadata to attacker.

facts.name: InFabric
facts.clarification: Indicates that Attacker and TargetDevice are part of the same Matter fabric.

facts.name: UnauthorizedAttribute
facts.clarification: Attribute or metadata that the attacker should not be able to read.

facts.name: LeakOccurred
facts.clarification: Represents that unauthorized attribute information was disclosed to the attacker.

rules.name: ProcessUnauthorizedDisclosure
rules.clarification: Because of improper in-fabric attribute isolation, the device leaks attribute information even though the attacker lacks proper privilege.

lemmas.name: UnauthorizedDisclosurePossible
lemmas.clarification: An in-fabric low-privileged node can obtain unauthorized device attribute information (footprinting).

initial_facts.clarification: Both attacker and target belong to the same Matter fabric; attacker is legitimate but low-privileged.
Target device has attributes requiring higher privilege and should not be disclosed.

title: Session establishment lock-up during replay of CASE Sigma1 messages
vulnerability_details: An issue in the CASE protocol implementation before Matter 1.1 allows an attacker to replay manipulated CASE Sigma1 messages to make the device unresponsive until power-cycled.

clarification.what_this_json_is_used_for: Map protocol steps to Tamarin model for replay detection and DoS liveness property violations.

title: KeySetRemove invalid parameter accepted causing DoS
cvss_score: Denial of Service (no CVSS vector published)
reproduction_notes: Devices accept KeySetRemove(0) or missing argument {} which should be invalid. Once malformed command executed, subsequent commands may error with 'Invalid CASE parameter' or 'No shared trusted root'.

steps.clarification: Attacker sends KeySetRemove with GroupKeySetID = 0 or empty parameter ({}). Valid range is [1..65534].
Device accepts invalid removal, corrupts group key state or removes key material in unexpected way.
Device begins returning session/CASE errors for future commands.

facts.name: InvalidRemoveAccepted
facts.clarification: Device accepted KeySetRemove with invalid GroupKeySetID (0 or {}).

facts.name: DoSState
facts.clarification: Final DoS state where device cannot process normal Matter commands.

rules.name: AcceptInvalidKeySetRemove
rules.clarification: KeySetRemove should validate GroupKeySetID ∈ [1..65534], but devices accept 0 or missing argument.

rules.name: TransitionToFailureState
rules.clarification: Invalid removal causes cryptographic/session state corruption leading to failure.

lemmas.name: InvalidKeySetRemoveCausesDoS
lemmas.clarification: Invalid KeySetRemove commands allow unauthenticated DoS on Matter devices.

initial_facts.clarification: A Matter device supporting GroupKeyManagement cluster exists initially.
